description: A visual example of 4-shot 2-class image classification showing two categories (e.g., dogs vs. cats) each with 4 labeled support images and one query image to classify
teaching_value: Concretizes the abstract N-shot K-class terminology by showing exactly what a support set looks like and how a query image relates to it, making the few-shot learning setup tangible
when_to_show: Show at the very start when introducing few-shot learning, before any math, to ground the student in what a 'task' and 'dataset as a sample' means
suggested_caption: 4-shot 2-class image classification: support set and query example.

description: Architecture diagram of a convolutional Siamese neural network showing two identical CNN branches processing a pair of images, sharing weights, and computing an L1 distance between embeddings to produce a similarity score
teaching_value: Illustrates the twin-network weight-sharing design and how pairwise similarity is computed from embeddings, clarifying why Siamese networks are suited for few-shot learning without retraining
when_to_show: Show when introducing metric-based meta-learning methods, specifically before explaining how the network is trained on pairs and generalizes to unseen classes
suggested_caption: Siamese CNN: twin weight-sharing branches compute embedding similarity.

description: Architecture diagram of Matching Networks showing an embedding function f for the query, embedding function g with bidirectional LSTM for support set items, attention-weighted sum over support labels to produce a prediction
teaching_value: Shows how attention over a support set replaces a parametric classifier, and how Full Contextual Embeddings (FCE) use an LSTM to make support embeddings context-aware, illustrating the key innovations over simpler metric methods
when_to_show: Show after Siamese networks to contrast fixed embeddings with context-dependent embeddings, and when explaining how attention replaces nearest-neighbor lookup
suggested_caption: Matching Networks: attention over support set embeddings for classification.

description: Architecture diagram of Relation Network for a 5-way 1-shot problem showing an embedding module that encodes query and support images, concatenation of embedding pairs, and a relation module (CNN) that outputs a relation score
teaching_value: Highlights the key difference from Siamese/Matching Networks: the distance/similarity function is itself learned (relation module) rather than fixed, showing how learned metrics can outperform hand-crafted ones
when_to_show: Show after Matching Networks to illustrate the progression from fixed to learned distance functions, emphasizing the two-module design
suggested_caption: Relation Network: learned relation module scores query-support embedding pairs.

description: Diagram of Prototypical Networks in both few-shot and zero-shot scenarios, showing how class prototype vectors are computed as mean embeddings of support examples, and how query points are classified by nearest prototype
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the prototype computation and nearest-centroid classification, and contrasts few-shot (prototype from examples) with zero-shot (prototype from class metadata), clarifying the generality of the approach
when_to_show: Show when explaining Prototypical Networks to illustrate how averaging embeddings creates class prototypes and how the same framework extends to zero-shot settings
suggested_caption: Prototypical Networks: class prototypes as mean embeddings, few- and zero-shot.

description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) showing a controller network connected to an external memory matrix M_t of size N×M, with read and write heads using soft attention over memory rows
teaching_value: Establishes the NTM architecture as a foundation for understanding MANN-based meta-learning, showing how external memory enables rapid storage and retrieval of new information beyond what internal RNN states allow
when_to_show: Show before explaining MANN for meta-learning to ensure students understand the base NTM architecture and why external memory is advantageous over internal RNN memory
suggested_caption: NTM architecture: controller with soft-attention read/write over external memory.

description: Diagram of the MANN meta-learning task setup showing how input x_t is presented at time t with the label y_{t-1} from the previous timestep, forcing the network to store inputs in memory and retrieve them when the delayed label arrives
teaching_value: Clarifies the deliberate temporal offset between input and label that forces the memory to retain information across timesteps, which is the key training trick enabling MANN to learn to memorize new task information rapidly
when_to_show: Show when explaining how MANN is adapted for meta-learning, specifically to illustrate why the label-delay trick forces the network to use its external memory effectively
suggested_caption: MANN meta-learning: labels delayed by one step to force memory use.

description: Diagram of a MLP combining slow weights (standard SGD-trained) and fast weights (generated by another network) via element-wise addition, showing how fast weights modulate the slow-weight network's activations
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the fast/slow weight distinction and how they are combined, making the abstract concept of one network predicting another network's parameters tangible for understanding MetaNet and related approaches
when_to_show: Show when introducing MetaNet's fast weight mechanism, before the full MetaNet architecture, to establish the conceptual building block of fast vs. slow weights
suggested_caption: Slow + fast weights combined element-wise in a MLP for rapid adaptation.

description: Full architecture diagram of MetaNet showing the base learner and meta-learner components, fast weight generation from loss gradients, task-level and example-level fast weight computation, and how they are injected back into the embedding and classification functions
teaching_value: Integrates all MetaNet components into one view, showing how fast weights flow from gradient signals through auxiliary networks back into the main model, helping students trace the full forward/backward pass of this complex architecture
when_to_show: Show after explaining fast weights and the individual MetaNet components, as a synthesis diagram to help students see how all parts connect in the full training loop
suggested_caption: MetaNet architecture: fast weights generated and injected for rapid task adaptation.

description: Diagram showing the interaction between the learner M_θ and the meta-learner R_Θ (LSTM-based optimizer), illustrating how the meta-learner receives gradients and loss from the learner and outputs updated parameters, across training episodes
teaching_value: Visualizes the two-level optimization loop where the LSTM meta-learner learns to update the learner's weights, making the analogy between LSTM cell state updates and gradient descent parameter updates concrete and traceable
when_to_show: Show when explaining LSTM-based meta-learners to illustrate how the meta-learner receives gradient information and produces parameter updates, and how training mimics test-time conditions
suggested_caption: LSTM meta-learner updates learner parameters using gradient and loss signals.

description: Diagram showing the meta-learner training process: an outer loop samples tasks from meta-train distribution, an inner loop updates learner parameters over T steps on D_train, and the final parameters theta_T are evaluated on D_test to update the meta-learner.
teaching_value: Visualizes the two-level (inner/outer) optimization structure of meta-learning, helping students understand how training mimics test-time adaptation and why the meta-learner is trained on held-out test splits.
when_to_show: When introducing the general meta-learning training paradigm before diving into specific algorithms like MAML; use to contrast inner-loop task adaptation with outer-loop meta-objective optimization.
suggested_caption: Meta-learner training: inner loop adapts, outer loop optimizes across tasks. (Finn et al., 2017)

description: Diagram of MAML showing a single initial parameter theta branching into multiple task-specific adapted parameters (theta_1*, theta_2*, theta_3*) after gradient steps, with the meta-update pulling theta toward a point that enables fast adaptation to all tasks.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the geometric intuition of MAML: finding an initialization in parameter space from which a few gradient steps reach good performance on any task, making the concept of 'fast adaptation' tangible.
when_to_show: When first introducing MAML; show before the mathematical formulation to build geometric intuition about why a shared initialization is valuable across tasks.
suggested_caption: MAML finds an initialization enabling fast adaptation to any task. (Finn et al., 2017)

description: Pseudocode of the general MAML algorithm showing: require p(tau), alpha, beta; sample tasks; for each task compute gradient and adapted parameters; update meta-parameters using test loss on adapted parameters with second-order gradients.
teaching_value: Provides the precise algorithmic steps of MAML including the second-order meta-gradient update, allowing students to implement or trace through the algorithm and understand the computational cost of second derivatives.
when_to_show: After explaining the geometric intuition of MAML; use when students need to understand implementation details or when comparing to FOMAML to highlight where second derivatives appear.
suggested_caption: MAML pseudocode: inner gradient steps then second-order meta-update. (Finn et al., 2017)

description: Pseudocode of the batched Reptile algorithm: initialize theta, repeatedly sample tasks, run k steps of SGD on each task to get W, then update theta toward W using step size epsilon.
teaching_value: Shows how Reptile simplifies meta-learning to a near-SGD procedure without second-order gradients, helping students contrast its simplicity with MAML and understand why k>1 steps make it non-trivially different from standard SGD.
when_to_show: When introducing Reptile after MAML; highlight the algorithmic simplicity and ask students to identify what makes it different from plain SGD (the k>1 inner steps).
suggested_caption: Batched Reptile: sample tasks, run SGD k steps, update toward adapted weights. (Nichol et al., 2018)

description: Geometric visualization showing two curved manifolds (W_tau1* and W_tau2*) representing optimal parameter regions for two tasks, with Reptile's iterative updates moving theta alternately closer to each manifold, converging toward their intersection.
teaching_value: Provides geometric intuition for why Reptile works: it moves parameters toward the intersection of task-optimal manifolds, making the abstract optimization objective concrete and visually memorable.
when_to_show: When explaining the theoretical justification for Reptile; use to help students visualize why alternating task updates lead to a generalizable initialization rather than overfitting to any single task.
suggested_caption: Reptile moves theta toward the intersection of task-optimal manifolds. (Nichol et al., 2018)

description: Side-by-side comparison diagram of Reptile vs FOMAML gradient updates in one meta-optimization loop, showing the direction and composition of gradient steps for each algorithm starting from the same initial theta.
teaching_value: Directly compares the gradient update directions of Reptile and FOMAML, helping students understand their mathematical relationship and why Reptile can be seen as an approximation to FOMAML through Taylor expansion analysis.
when_to_show: When analyzing the theoretical connection between Reptile and FOMAML; use alongside the Taylor expansion derivation to help students see how the two algorithms relate geometrically and mathematically.
suggested_caption: Reptile vs FOMAML: comparing meta-gradient directions in one update loop. (Lee, Yoonho)
